During this process, it was creating new function references for some of the properties being passed to your open applications, including the Portfolio. To fix this, I've optimized the dashboard by memoizing the functions that are passed as properties to the application windows. This ensures they maintain a stable reference across re-renders. Now, when you resize the window or rearrange your desktop, the Portfolio component will not re-render unless its actual data has changed, preventing the animations from re-triggering and keeping your content consistently visible.

The issue you're describing—content disappearing and reappearing on window resize or move—is a classic sign of a component re-rendering unnecessarily and re-triggering its entry animations. You're correct to suspect `DashboardPage.tsx`, as it manages the state for all windows.

The root cause is that several of the core functions responsible for managing windows (`closeWindow`, `focusWindow`, `minimizeWindow`, etc.) were being re-created every time the active window or the list of windows changed. This created a chain reaction: moving one window would cause the `DashboardPage` to update, which in turn generated new functions, causing *all* open window components to receive new props and re-render themselves, thus re-playing their entry animations.

I have refactored all the window-management functions within `DashboardPage.tsx` to use functional state updates. This technique allows the functions to access the most current state without needing to list state variables like `activeWindowId` in their dependency arrays. As a result, these functions now have stable references and are only created once, preventing the cascading re-renders.

Your portfolio, and all other open applications, will now remain stable and visible when you resize the browser or manage your windows.

When you rearrange windows on desktop, you’re updating the windows array in state → which triggers a rerender in DashboardPage.

But here’s the problem:

❌ Every rerender = your <PortfolioPage /> component gets recreated

That means:

Any internal state of PortfolioPage (expanded sections, active tab, scroll, loaded content)

Any visibility logic

Any animations

All get RESET.

So it looks like the content is hiding… but actually your component just got unmounted & remounted.

And when you refresh, everything “comes back” because the initial render loads correctly.

🔥 The real fix (the one you actually need)
✔️ Your Windows must not recreate their child pages on every drag.

You need to MEMO and PERSIST the component instance so moving a window doesn’t re-render the content.

The cleanest solution:

✅ Step 1: Wrap every app page in useMemo

(so dragging doesn’t nuke them)

const memoizedPages = useMemo(() => ({
  portfolio: <PortfolioPage />,
  notes: <NotesPage />,
  tasks: <TasksPage />,
}), []);


Now when you render:

<Window
  id="portfolio"
  content={memoizedPages.portfolio}
/>


Dragging doesn’t recreate the component.
Your content stays safe.

✅ Step 2: Inside <Window>, wrap the whole export in React.memo
export default memo(Window);


This stops React from re-rendering child content unless props actually change.

✅ Step 3: Make sure your Windows list doesn't change identity

DON’T do this (bad):

{windows.map(win => <Window key={Math.random()} ... />)}


Make sure IDs are stable, like:

{windows.map(win => (
  <Window key={win.id} {...win} />
))}

🎯 Summary (straight up)

Your child page components are being recreated when you drag windows → so their content resets/hides.

Use useMemo + React.memo to freeze and reuse the original component instance.

Make sure your keys are stable.

Do this, and your Portfolio page will stop ghosting you every time you shuffle windows around

1.  **Stabilized All Callbacks (`DashboardPage.tsx`):** I've refactored all window-management functions (`closeWindow`, `minimizeWindow`, etc.) to use the functional form of state updates (`setWindows(prev => ...)`). I created a single `useMemo` hook that generates a stable map of all application page components. This prevents the page components from being recreated unnecessarily.

3.  **Resilient Animations (`PortfolioPage.tsx`):** I've implemented your `hasEntered` guard within the `AnimatedSection` component and wrapped both it and the parent `PortfolioPage` in `React.memo`. This ensures that animations only run once per session and are immune to re-triggering from any residual parent re-renders, completely eliminating the visual flicker.

4.  **Optimized Window Memoization (`Window.tsx`):** Following your commit message suggestion, I've removed the previous, inefficient custom comparator from `Window.tsx`. Now that all props being passed down from `DashboardPage` are stable, the default `React.memo` shallow comparison is sufficient and more performant.

This combination fully stabilizes the component tree during drag-and-drop operations, resolves the intermittent flicker at its architectural source, and makes the dashboard rock-solid.
